evals.name: hasmany-postgres
evals.prompt: I have a Rust app using the Toasty ORM with PostgreSQL. I want a User model that has many Post models, and each Post belongs to one User. Show me both model structs with the right Toasty attributes, then show me a snippet that creates one user with three posts in a single call. Explain any non-obvious attribute choices.
evals.expected_output: A correct pair of #[derive(toasty::Model)] structs with #[has_many] on User and #[belongs_to(key=user_id, references=id)] on Post; FK declared as a plain field; toasty::create! macro used with nested array of posts; .exec(&mut db).await? at the call site.

evals.name: dynamodb-filter
evals.prompt: My Toasty model targets DynamoDB. The model has `id` (the key) and `email`. I tried calling `User::filter_by_email(&db, "x@y.com").exec(&mut db).await?` and Rust says the method doesn't exist. Why doesn't this compile, and what should I do instead?
evals.expected_output: Explanation that Toasty does not abstract the database — query methods are only generated for access patterns the target driver can index. DynamoDB cannot filter by an arbitrary non-key field without a scan; the answer should suggest either adding a Global Secondary Index (or marking the field as an index), making the field part of the key, or accepting a scan. Should point at the dynamodb guide page.

evals.name: query-pipeline
evals.prompt: I'm reading the toasty source code under submodules/toasty/ and I see the query engine produces an action sequence like `$0 = ExecSQL(...)`, `$1 = NestedMerge($0, ...)`. Walk me through what phase of the compilation pipeline produces this representation, where it lives in the source tree, and what `NestedMerge` is solving for.
evals.expected_output: Names the four phases: Simplify → Lower (to HIR) → Plan (to MIR DAG) → Exec. Identifies plan.rs (the Plan phase) as the one that produces the numbered-slot action sequence (MIR). Explains NestedMerge is introduced to break cycles in the HIR dependency graph when converting to a DAG. Mentions exec.rs as the interpreter and the only phase that touches the driver.
